title: Stridor — Assessment
oneLiner: Acute upper airway obstruction — a medical emergency requiring immediate assessment for cause and patency.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Complete airway obstruction** (foreign body, anaphylaxis, laryngeal oedema, epiglottitis)  
  *Single finding:* **Silent chest** – no stridor, no breath sounds, because no airflow. This is a pre-terminal sign.
- **Tracheal compression** (mediastinal mass, retrosternal goitre, retropharyngeal abscess)  
  *Single finding:* **Positional stridor** – worse with lying flat, relieved by leaning forward (tripod) or Pemberton’s sign (facial congestion when arms raised).
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Noisy breathing (inspiratory stridor – louder in neck; biphasic if severe)  
  - Hoarse voice, barking cough (croup) or silent cough (foreign body)  
  - Drooling, dysphagia (epiglottitis, retropharyngeal abscess)  
  - Sudden onset (foreign body, anaphylaxis) vs gradual (infective, tumour)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Tripod position, tracheal tug, intercostal/supraclavicular recession  
  - Use of accessory muscles, nasal flaring  
  - Ability to speak in full sentences (danger if unable)  
  - Oxygen saturation, heart rate, respiratory rate  
  - Absence of fever vs high fever (toxic appearance suggests infection)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Croup (viral laryngotracheobronchitis)** – barking cough, age 6m–3y, low-grade fever, worse at night
- **Epiglottitis** – acute, drooling, tripod, high fever, muffled voice, no cough; rare since HiB vaccine but still possible (e.g. other bacteria)
- **Foreign body aspiration** – sudden onset, unilateral wheeze/reduced air entry, choking episode, child/elderly
- **Anaphylaxis** – urticaria, angioedema (lips/tongue), hypotension, itching, rapid onset
- **Bacterial tracheitis** – toxic, high fever, productive cough, thick secretions, stridor worse after cough
- **Retropharyngeal abscess** – fever, stiff neck, dysphagia, bulging posterior pharyngeal wall
- **Laryngomalacia** – congenital, intermittent, worst when supine, benign, resolves by 2 years
- **Vocal cord dysfunction** – female, young adult, triggers (stress/exercise), paradoxical movement, normal O2
- **Mediastinal mass** – persistent, positional, Pemberton’s sign, superior vena cava syndrome
- **Post-extubation stridor** – history of recent intubation, laryngeal oedema
- **Splitter:** **Barking cough** → croup vs **Drooling + tripod** → epiglottitis (treat as epiglottitis until proven)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Assess airway patency (look, listen, feel) – do not disturb child/parents  
  - Pulse oximetry, capnography if available  
  - Do NOT examine throat with tongue depressor if epiglottitis suspected  
  - If foreign body suspected and patient stable: chest X-ray (PA + lateral) for radiopaque object, hyperinflation, atelectasis  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, CRP, blood cultures (if febrile)  
  - U&E, lactate (if shock or severe distress)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: **Lateral neck X-ray** (caution: only if stable – may precipitate complete obstruction; watch during positioning)  
  - Second-line: **CT neck/chest** (if mass/abscess suspected, only after airway secured)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Flexible nasendoscopy** by ENT – gold standard for laryngeal view in stable patient  
  - **Direct laryngoscopy** under anaesthetic – definitive in unstable or uncooperative patient  
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Direct visualization of the larynx (by senior anaesthetist/ENT)** – omission is a serious error; delay can lead to complete obstruction and arrest.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Keep patient calm; allow position of comfort (upright, tripod)  
  - High-flow oxygen via mask (no bag if child – avoid agitation)  
  - Call **senior anaesthetist/ENT/paediatric reg** immediately  
  - Nebulised **adrenaline (1:1000)** 0.5 mL/kg (max 5 mL) diluted with 3 mL saline for croup/post-extubation oedema  
  - **Heliox** (helium 70% / oxygen 30%) if available – reduces airflow resistance (temporising bridge)  
  - **Steroids:** Dexamethasone 0.15–0.6 mg/kg (max 10 mg) oral/IM – single dose for croup  
  - For anaphylaxis: **IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000)** into anterolateral thigh; repeat every 5 min if needed  
  - If complete obstruction: **Heimlich manoeuvre** (conscious) or **cricothyroidotomy** (unconscious, fails airway manoeuvres) – do NOT do blind finger sweep  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Monitor in **HDU/ICU** if stridor at rest, biphasic stridor, or any respiratory distress  
  - Continue steroids: dexamethasone 0.15–0.6 mg/kg daily for 2–3 days or switch to prednisolone 1–2 mg/kg  
  - Monitor for worsening: repeat nebulised adrenaline as needed (max every 1–2h)  
  - Treat underlying cause: antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole if epiglottitis/tracheitis), ENT removal of foreign body, drainage of abscess  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Refer to ENT for recurrent stridor (laryngomalacia, vocal cord dysfunction, subglottic stenosis)  
  - Complete resolution often with growth (laryngomalacia) or after definitive treatment  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **NBM** until airway assessment and safe swallow confirmed  
  - **IV fluids** (0.9% sodium chloride) at maintenance rate if NBM  
  - **VTE prophylaxis** (enoxaparin 20–40 mg SC) only if not bleeding and admitted >24h  
  - **Observations:** continuous monitoring (NEWS2) initially, then hourly once stable; escalate if NEWS2 ≥5 or respiratory rate >30
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Dexamethasone** (croup): 0.15–0.6 mg/kg (max 10 mg) PO/IM/IV – single dose, repeat after 24h if needed  
- **Nebulised adrenaline (1:1000):** 0.5 mL/kg (max 5 mL) diluted with 3 mL saline – give over 10–15 min via nebuliser; may repeat every 1–2h  
- **Adrenaline IM (anaphylaxis):** 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1000) into anterolateral thigh – children <12: 0.01 mL/kg (max 0.5 mL)  
- **Ceftriaxone** (epiglottitis/tracheitis): 50–100 mg/kg IV once daily (max 4 g) plus **metronidazole** 7.5 mg/kg IV TDS if abscess  
- **Key contraindications:** Dexamethasone – avoid in active untreated infection? (No, it’s indicated for croup). Adrenaline – avoid in hypertension/thyrotoxicosis unless life-threatening  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Ceftriaxone: adjust in severe renal failure (dose after dialysis). Dexamethasone: no dose adjustment needed  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving oral dexamethasone to a stridulous patient unable to swallow** – risk of aspiration and complete obstruction. Use IM/IV route instead.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Complete airway obstruction → hypoxic arrest  
  - Aspiration pneumonia (if swallowing compromised)  
  - Post-obstructive pulmonary oedema (after relief of obstruction)  
  - Bradycardia (hypoxia)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Subglottic stenosis (from prolonged intubation, burns, or recurrent croup)  
  - Failure to thrive (chronic obstruction in infants)  
  - Vocal cord paresis
sections.Prognosis:
- Croup: self-limiting; <5% require admission, <1% intubation. Full recovery in days.  
- Epiglottitis: <1% mortality if managed promptly with airway protection and antibiotics.  
- Foreign body aspiration: outcomes excellent if removed early; delayed → pneumonia, lung abscess.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
1. **Use nebulised adrenaline early in croup** – it reduces work of breathing and may avoid intubation.  
2. **Call senior/anaesthetist before performing any imaging** if stridor at rest – do not send the patient to radiology alone.  
3. **Keep the patient with their parents/carers** – calm environment reduces oxygen consumption and airway agitation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
1. **Do not lie the patient flat** – worsens obstruction; always keep upright or tripod.  
2. **Do not sedate a stridulous child** – may precipitate airway collapse and loss of protective reflexes.  
3. **Do not delay senior review to get tests** – urgent airway assessment is the priority; bloods and X-ray come after.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Stridor at rest or worsening breathing  
  - Drooling, difficulty swallowing, or hoarseness  
  - Fever not settling after 48h (for infective causes)  
- **Follow-up:** GP at 48–72h or ENT clinic in 1–2 weeks if recurrent episodes
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ENT/anaesthetist immediately if:**  
  - Stridor at rest, biphasic stridor, or hypoxia (SpO₂ <92% despite O₂)  
  - Unable to speak in full sentences, tripod positioning, drooling  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or any deterioration in conscious level  
  - Complete obstruction – call crash team and prepare for surgical airway

rawMarkdown:
## Stridor — Assessment
**One-liner:** Acute upper airway obstruction — a medical emergency requiring immediate assessment for cause and patency.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Complete airway obstruction** (foreign body, anaphylaxis, laryngeal oedema, epiglottitis)  
  *Single finding:* **Silent chest** – no stridor, no breath sounds, because no airflow. This is a pre-terminal sign.
- **Tracheal compression** (mediastinal mass, retrosternal goitre, retropharyngeal abscess)  
  *Single finding:* **Positional stridor** – worse with lying flat, relieved by leaning forward (tripod) or Pemberton’s sign (facial congestion when arms raised).

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Noisy breathing (inspiratory stridor – louder in neck; biphasic if severe)  
  - Hoarse voice, barking cough (croup) or silent cough (foreign body)  
  - Drooling, dysphagia (epiglottitis, retropharyngeal abscess)  
  - Sudden onset (foreign body, anaphylaxis) vs gradual (infective, tumour)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Tripod position, tracheal tug, intercostal/supraclavicular recession  
  - Use of accessory muscles, nasal flaring  
  - Ability to speak in full sentences (danger if unable)  
  - Oxygen saturation, heart rate, respiratory rate  
  - Absence of fever vs high fever (toxic appearance suggests infection)  

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Croup (viral laryngotracheobronchitis)** – barking cough, age 6m–3y, low-grade fever, worse at night
- **Epiglottitis** – acute, drooling, tripod, high fever, muffled voice, no cough; rare since HiB vaccine but still possible (e.g. other bacteria)
- **Foreign body aspiration** – sudden onset, unilateral wheeze/reduced air entry, choking episode, child/elderly
- **Anaphylaxis** – urticaria, angioedema (lips/tongue), hypotension, itching, rapid onset
- **Bacterial tracheitis** – toxic, high fever, productive cough, thick secretions, stridor worse after cough
- **Retropharyngeal abscess** – fever, stiff neck, dysphagia, bulging posterior pharyngeal wall
- **Laryngomalacia** – congenital, intermittent, worst when supine, benign, resolves by 2 years
- **Vocal cord dysfunction** – female, young adult, triggers (stress/exercise), paradoxical movement, normal O2
- **Mediastinal mass** – persistent, positional, Pemberton’s sign, superior vena cava syndrome
- **Post-extubation stridor** – history of recent intubation, laryngeal oedema
- **Splitter:** **Barking cough** → croup vs **Drooling + tripod** → epiglottitis (treat as epiglottitis until proven)

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Assess airway patency (look, listen, feel) – do not disturb child/parents  
  - Pulse oximetry, capnography if available  
  - Do NOT examine throat with tongue depressor if epiglottitis suspected  
  - If foreign body suspected and patient stable: chest X-ray (PA + lateral) for radiopaque object, hyperinflation, atelectasis  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, CRP, blood cultures (if febrile)  
  - U&E, lactate (if shock or severe distress)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: **Lateral neck X-ray** (caution: only if stable – may precipitate complete obstruction; watch during positioning)  
  - Second-line: **CT neck/chest** (if mass/abscess suspected, only after airway secured)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Flexible nasendoscopy** by ENT – gold standard for laryngeal view in stable patient  
  - **Direct laryngoscopy** under anaesthetic – definitive in unstable or uncooperative patient  
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Direct visualization of the larynx (by senior anaesthetist/ENT)** – omission is a serious error; delay can lead to complete obstruction and arrest.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Keep patient calm; allow position of comfort (upright, tripod)  
  - High-flow oxygen via mask (no bag if child – avoid agitation)  
  - Call **senior anaesthetist/ENT/paediatric reg** immediately  
  - Nebulised **adrenaline (1:1000)** 0.5 mL/kg (max 5 mL) diluted with 3 mL saline for croup/post-extubation oedema  
  - **Heliox** (helium 70% / oxygen 30%) if available – reduces airflow resistance (temporising bridge)  
  - **Steroids:** Dexamethasone 0.15–0.6 mg/kg (max 10 mg) oral/IM – single dose for croup  
  - For anaphylaxis: **IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000)** into anterolateral thigh; repeat every 5 min if needed  
  - If complete obstruction: **Heimlich manoeuvre** (conscious) or **cricothyroidotomy** (unconscious, fails airway manoeuvres) – do NOT do blind finger sweep  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Monitor in **HDU/ICU** if stridor at rest, biphasic stridor, or any respiratory distress  
  - Continue steroids: dexamethasone 0.15–0.6 mg/kg daily for 2–3 days or switch to prednisolone 1–2 mg/kg  
  - Monitor for worsening: repeat nebulised adrenaline as needed (max every 1–2h)  
  - Treat underlying cause: antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole if epiglottitis/tracheitis), ENT removal of foreign body, drainage of abscess  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Refer to ENT for recurrent stridor (laryngomalacia, vocal cord dysfunction, subglottic stenosis)  
  - Complete resolution often with growth (laryngomalacia) or after definitive treatment  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **NBM** until airway assessment and safe swallow confirmed  
  - **IV fluids** (0.9% sodium chloride) at maintenance rate if NBM  
  - **VTE prophylaxis** (enoxaparin 20–40 mg SC) only if not bleeding and admitted >24h  
  - **Observations:** continuous monitoring (NEWS2) initially, then hourly once stable; escalate if NEWS2 ≥5 or respiratory rate >30

### Drugs & Doses
- **Dexamethasone** (croup): 0.15–0.6 mg/kg (max 10 mg) PO/IM/IV – single dose, repeat after 24h if needed  
- **Nebulised adrenaline (1:1000):** 0.5 mL/kg (max 5 mL) diluted with 3 mL saline – give over 10–15 min via nebuliser; may repeat every 1–2h  
- **Adrenaline IM (anaphylaxis):** 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1000) into anterolateral thigh – children <12: 0.01 mL/kg (max 0.5 mL)  
- **Ceftriaxone** (epiglottitis/tracheitis): 50–100 mg/kg IV once daily (max 4 g) plus **metronidazole** 7.5 mg/kg IV TDS if abscess  
- **Key contraindications:** Dexamethasone – avoid in active untreated infection? (No, it’s indicated for croup). Adrenaline – avoid in hypertension/thyrotoxicosis unless life-threatening  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Ceftriaxone: adjust in severe renal failure (dose after dialysis). Dexamethasone: no dose adjustment needed  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving oral dexamethasone to a stridulous patient unable to swallow** – risk of aspiration and complete obstruction. Use IM/IV route instead.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Complete airway obstruction → hypoxic arrest  
  - Aspiration pneumonia (if swallowing compromised)  
  - Post-obstructive pulmonary oedema (after relief of obstruction)  
  - Bradycardia (hypoxia)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Subglottic stenosis (from prolonged intubation, burns, or recurrent croup)  
  - Failure to thrive (chronic obstruction in infants)  
  - Vocal cord paresis

### Prognosis
- Croup: self-limiting; <5% require admission, <1% intubation. Full recovery in days.  
- Epiglottitis: <1% mortality if managed promptly with airway protection and antibiotics.  
- Foreign body aspiration: outcomes excellent if removed early; delayed → pneumonia, lung abscess.

### Pearls (positive actions)
1. **Use nebulised adrenaline early in croup** – it reduces work of breathing and may avoid intubation.  
2. **Call senior/anaesthetist before performing any imaging** if stridor at rest – do not send the patient to radiology alone.  
3. **Keep the patient with their parents/carers** – calm environment reduces oxygen consumption and airway agitation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
1. **Do not lie the patient flat** – worsens obstruction; always keep upright or tripod.  
2. **Do not sedate a stridulous child** – may precipitate airway collapse and loss of protective reflexes.  
3. **Do not delay senior review to get tests** – urgent airway assessment is the priority; bloods and X-ray come after.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Stridor at rest or worsening breathing  
  - Drooling, difficulty swallowing, or hoarseness  
  - Fever not settling after 48h (for infective causes)  
- **Follow-up:** GP at 48–72h or ENT clinic in 1–2 weeks if recurrent episodes

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ENT/anaesthetist immediately if:**  
  - Stridor at rest, biphasic stridor, or hypoxia (SpO₂ <92% despite O₂)  
  - Unable to speak in full sentences, tripod positioning, drooling  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or any deterioration in conscious level  
  - Complete obstruction – call crash team and prepare for surgical airway
